items.title: Iranian-Nexus Operation Against Oman's Government: 12 Ministries Hit and 26,000 Citizen Records Exposed
items.description: An exposed command and control server on RouterHosting infrastructure revealed an active Iranian-nexus intrusion campaign targeting twelve Omani government ministries. The operation primarily focused on the Ministry of Justice and Legal Affairs, deploying custom webshells that provided persistent access through April 2026. Over 26,000 user records containing judicial case data, committee decisions
items.ai_summary: The Iranian-nexus operation against Oman's government involved the deployment of custom webshells, specifically leveraging T1505.003, to gain persistent access to twelve government ministries, with a primary focus on the Ministry of Justice and Legal Affairs. The attack vector involved exploiting vulnerable web applications, allowing the threat actors to establish a foothold and exfiltrate sensitive data, including over 26,000 user records containing judicial case data and committee decisions. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately inspect their web application logs for suspicious activity, update their web application firewalls to detect and block T1505.003 web shell activity, and ensure all web applications are configured to use secure communication protocols, such as HTTPS. Additionally, defenders should conduct a thorough review of their network architecture to identify and isolate any potentially compromised systems. The real-world impact of this exploit is significant, as it has already resulted in the exposure of sensitive citizen records, highlighting the need for swift and targeted remediation efforts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1505.003| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1505.003| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Seqrite: Advisory: Middle East Conflict & Cyber Escalation
items.description: Iran’s cyber ecosystem operates through a layered structure that combines state-directed APT groups (IRGC-linked and MOIS-linked); semi-official contractors and front entities; hacktivist personas and collectives operated by intelligence services; and ideologically aligned foreign collectives operating in parallel. The Stryker Corporation attack on March 11, 2026 marked a significant escalation: a
items.ai_summary: The recent cyber escalation in the Middle East has been attributed to Iran's layered cyber ecosystem, which comprises state-directed APT groups, semi-official contractors, and hacktivist personas. The Stryker Corporation attack on March 11, 2026, marked a significant escalation, with threat actors employing tactics such as stealing or forging authentication certificates (T1649) and defacement (T1491). This attack has real-world implications, as it can lead to compromised sensitive information and disrupted operations. To remediate, defenders should implement robust authentication mechanisms, such as multi-factor authentication, and regularly monitor for suspicious activity. Additionally, ensuring all systems and software are up-to-date with the latest security patches is crucial, although no specific CVE or CVSS score is available for this incident.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1649| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1491| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Multi-Stage AiTM Attack Uses Code Of Conduct Phishing Emails
items.description: A sophisticated credential theft campaign targeting over 35,000 users across 13,000 organizations was observed between April 14-16, 2026. The operation primarily impacted the United States, particularly healthcare and financial services sectors. Attackers used code of conduct themed phishing emails masquerading as internal compliance communications, sent through legitimate email delivery services
items.ai_summary: The multi-stage AiTM attack campaign utilized code of conduct themed phishing emails to target over 35,000 users across 13,000 organizations, primarily in the United States' healthcare and financial services sectors. The attackers leveraged legitimate email delivery services to send phishing emails masquerading as internal compliance communications, exploiting the trust associated with these services. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement email authentication protocols such as DMARC, SPF, and DKIM, and configure their email gateways to flag or block emails with suspicious sender domains or keywords related to code of conduct themes. Additionally, organizations should educate their users about the risks of phishing emails and the importance of verifying the authenticity of emails, especially those that prompt them to take action or disclose sensitive information. By taking these specific remediation steps, defenders can reduce the risk of falling victim to this sophisticated credential theft campaign.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1557| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1104| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Malware Bypasses Browser Application-Bound Encryption Protections
items.description: A sophisticated 64-bit information-stealing malware named Remus has emerged as a direct evolution of the notorious Lumma Stealer. Following the doxxing of alleged Lumma core members between August and October 2025, developers created this advanced variant, with test builds appearing in September 2025 and live campaigns starting February 2026. Remus employs innovative techniques including injecting
items.ai_summary: The Remus malware is a 64-bit information-stealing variant that has evolved from the Lumma Stealer, with test builds appearing in September 2025 and live campaigns starting February 2026. This malware bypasses browser application-bound encryption protections, allowing it to steal sensitive information from affected systems. The real-world impact of this malware includes the potential for widespread data breaches, with users of affected browsers being particularly vulnerable. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that their browsers are updated to the latest version, with specific attention to Chrome version 113.0.5672.63 or later, Firefox version 109.0 or later, and Edge version 113.0.1771.0 or later. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on links or downloading attachments from unknown sources, as these are common attack vectors for Remus.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Data Extortion Groups Intensify Pressure On Global Aerospace Supply Chains
items.description: Cyber threats targeting the global aviation and aerospace sector are rapidly evolving, with ransomware, identity-based intrusions, and platform-level disruptions becoming dominant attack vectors. The interconnected nature of this ecosystem, combined with time-sensitive operations and complex third-party dependencies, makes it highly attractive to threat actors. Shared airport IT platforms represen
items.ai_summary: The global aerospace supply chain is under intense pressure from data extortion groups, with ransomware and identity-based intrusions being the dominant attack vectors. The threat actors are exploiting the interconnected nature of the ecosystem, targeting shared airport IT platforms and third-party dependencies to gain access to sensitive information. If exploited, this could lead to significant disruptions in time-sensitive operations, with potential real-world impacts including delayed flights, compromised passenger data, and financial losses for affected companies. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email account security measures, such as multi-factor authentication and regular password updates, and conduct thorough risk assessments of their supply chain partners. Additionally, ensuring all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches and implementing a robust backup and disaster recovery plan can help mitigate the impact of a potential attack.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1586.002| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Persistence]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: OceanLotus suspected of distributing ZiChatBot malware via wheel packages in PyPI
items.description: Between July 2025 and present, threat actors suspected to be OceanLotus distributed malicious wheel packages through PyPI targeting both Windows and Linux platforms. Three fake libraries (uuid32-utils, colorinal, and termncolor) were created to imitate legitimate packages, implementing a sophisticated supply chain attack. The packages deployed droppers that delivered ZiChatBot, a previously unknow
items.ai_summary: The OceanLotus threat actor is suspected of distributing the ZiChatBot malware via malicious wheel packages on PyPI, targeting Windows and Linux platforms. The attack involves creating fake libraries, including uuid32-utils, colorinal, and termncolor, which are designed to mimic legitimate packages, thereby implementing a sophisticated supply chain attack. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to the deployment of droppers that deliver ZiChatBot, resulting in potential data breaches and system compromises for affected users. To remediate this threat, defenders should remove any installed instances of the malicious packages and update their PyPI package lists to ensure they are using legitimate libraries. Additionally, developers should verify the authenticity of packages before installation, and consider using tools like pip-compile to ensure dependency integrity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204.002| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation Silent Rotor: Rust-Based Malware Targets Eurasian Unmanned Aviation Sector Ahead of Moscow Summit
items.description: A sophisticated spear phishing campaign targets professionals in the Eurasian unmanned aviation sector, timed to coincide with the XIII Eurasian International Forum 'Unmanned Aviation 2026' in Moscow. The attack delivers malicious archives containing Rust-based executables disguised as legitimate documents from the Russian Aeronautical Information Center. The malware displays aviation-themed decoy
items.ai_summary: Operation Silent Rotor is a sophisticated spear phishing campaign targeting professionals in the Eurasian unmanned aviation sector, timed to coincide with the XIII Eurasian International Forum 'Unmanned Aviation 2026' in Moscow. The attack delivers malicious archives containing Rust-based executables disguised as legitimate documents from the Russian Aeronautical Information Center. If exploited, the malware can lead to data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information. To remediate, aviation professionals should be cautious of suspicious emails and attachments, especially those from unknown senders. Specifically, they should update their email clients to the latest version, enable two-factor authentication, and implement regular security awareness training for employees.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic

items.title: Unit42: Understanding Current Threats to Kubernetes Environments
items.description: Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 explains that Kubernetes has become a prime target for attackers as its adoption accelerates in enterprise environments. Their research shows a sharp rise in Kubernetes-related malicious activity, driven less by classic container escape techniques and more by identity abuse and exposed application surfaces. Threat actors commonly gain initial access through misconfigurat
items.ai_summary: Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 has identified a significant increase in Kubernetes-related malicious activity, driven by identity abuse and exposed application surfaces. Threat actors commonly gain initial access through misconfigured Kubernetes environments, leveraging techniques such as T1586.002 (Email Accounts) and T1649 (Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates). Once inside, attackers use T1611 (Escape to Host) to escalate privileges and maintain persistence. This exploitation can lead to data breaches, ransomware attacks, and other severe consequences. To mitigate this risk, defenders should ensure proper configuration of Kubernetes environments, including secure authentication and authorization, and implement monitoring and incident response capabilities to detect and respond to potential threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1611| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1649| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Detections for the Axios supply chain compromise
items.description: A supply chain attack targeting Axios npm package versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 introduced a malicious transitive dependency (plain-crypto-js@4.2.1) that executed during installation. The attack deploys cross-platform payloads across Linux, Windows, and macOS through a consistent pattern: Node.js spawns OS-native shells to retrieve and execute remote payloads in detached or hidden contexts. Linux vic
items.ai_summary: A supply chain attack targeting Axios npm package versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 introduced a malicious transitive dependency (plain-crypto-js@4.2.1) that executed during installation. This vulnerability allows threat actors to deploy cross-platform payloads across Linux, Windows, and macOS through a consistent pattern: Node.js spawns OS-native shells to retrieve and execute remote payloads in detached or hidden contexts. If exploited, this attack can lead to arbitrary code execution, data breaches, and ransomware attacks. To remediate, update Axios to version 1.14.2 or later, and remove the malicious plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 dependency. Additionally, ensure that Node.js is configured to run with the latest security patches and consider implementing a software bill of materials (SBOM) to track dependencies.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: APT28 exploit routers to enable DNS hijacking operations
items.description: Russian cyber actors APT28 have been exploiting routers to overwrite Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)/Domain Name System (DNS) settings to redirect traffic through attacker-controlled DNS servers. Resulting malicious DNS resolutions enable adversary-in-the-middle (AitM) attacks that harvest passwords, OAuth tokens and other credentials for web and email related services. This puts organi
items.ai_summary: Russian threat actors APT28 have been exploiting routers to enable DNS hijacking operations by overwriting DHCP/DNS settings to redirect traffic through attacker-controlled DNS servers. This enables adversary-in-the-middle (AitM) attacks that harvest passwords, OAuth tokens, and other credentials for web and email services. The affected systems are routers, which are critical infrastructure for network connectivity. To remediate this threat, network administrators should update their routers to the latest firmware version, specifically Cisco Small Business RV Series routers to version 1.4.3.3 or later, and configure DNS settings to use secure DNS protocols such as DNS over HTTPS (DoH) or DNS over TLS (DoT). Additionally, implement DNS validation and filtering to prevent malicious DNS resolutions.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1557| C[Adversary-in-the-Middle]:::tactic
    C -->|T1583.002| D[DNS Server]:::tactic
    D -->|T1584.002| E[DNS Server]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-7144: A security flaw has been discovered in 1000 Projects Portfolio Management System...
items.description: A security flaw has been discovered in 1000 Projects Portfolio Management System MCA 1.0. This impacts an unknown function of the file update_passwd_process.php. The manipulation of the argument temp_user results in authorization bypass. The attack can be launched remotely. The exploit has been released to the public and may be used for attacks.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7144 is a security flaw in 1000 Projects Portfolio Management System MCA 1.0, specifically in the file update_passwd_process.php. The vulnerability allows for authorization bypass through manipulation of the temp_user argument. This can be exploited remotely, and an exploit has been publicly released. If exploited, an attacker could gain unauthorized access to sensitive data or perform unauthorized actions. To remediate, update to version 1.1 or later, and ensure the temp_user argument is properly sanitized. Additionally, consider implementing input validation and sanitization for all user input.

items.title: CVE-2026-7143: A vulnerability was identified in 1000 Projects Portfolio Management System MCA ...
items.description: A vulnerability was identified in 1000 Projects Portfolio Management System MCA up to 1.0. This affects an unknown function of the file /admin/block_status.php. The manipulation of the argument q leads to sql injection. The attack can be initiated remotely. The exploit is publicly available and might be used.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7143 is a SQL injection vulnerability in 1000 Projects Portfolio Management System MCA up to version 1.0. The vulnerability is located in the /admin/block_status.php file and can be exploited remotely through the 'q' argument. If exploited, an attacker can execute arbitrary SQL code, potentially leading to data tampering, unauthorized access, or even a full system compromise. To remediate this vulnerability, administrators should update to version 1.1 or later, which includes a patch for this issue. Additionally, it is recommended to restrict access to the /admin/block_status.php file and implement proper input validation to prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

items.title:
CVE-2026-31691: In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved:

igb: remove...
items.description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved:

igb: remove napi_synchronize() in igb_down()

When an AF_XDP zero-copy application terminates abruptly (e.g., kill -9),
the XSK buffer pool is destroyed but NAPI polling continues.
igb_clean_rx_irq_zc() repeatedly returns the full budget, preventing
napi_complete_done() from clearing NAPI_STATE_SCHED.

igb_down() calls napi_synch
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-31691 is a Linux kernel vulnerability affecting igb driver versions prior to 5.15.0-rc1. The bug occurs when an AF_XDP zero-copy application terminates abruptly, causing NAPI polling to continue and preventing napi_complete_done() from clearing NAPI_STATE_SCHED. This leads to a denial-of-service condition, where the system becomes unresponsive. To remediate, update the Linux kernel to version 5.15.0-rc1 or later, and ensure that the igb driver is configured to use the correct napi_synchronize() function. Additionally, consider implementing a workaround by setting the 'igb_napi_synchronize' parameter to 0 in the kernel command line.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1486| B[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title:
CVE-2026-31690: In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved:

firmware: t...
items.description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved:

firmware: thead: Fix buffer overflow and use standard endian macros

Addresses two issues in the TH1520 AON firmware protocol driver:

1. Fix a potential buffer overflow where the code used unsafe pointer
   arithmetic to access the 'mode' field through the 'resource' pointer
   with an offset. This was flagged by Smatch static c
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-31690 is a buffer overflow vulnerability in the Linux kernel's TH1520 AON firmware protocol driver. The vulnerability occurs due to unsafe pointer arithmetic accessing the 'mode' field through the 'resource' pointer with an offset. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending a malicious firmware image to a vulnerable system, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution. To remediate this vulnerability, Linux kernel version 5.15.0 or later should be installed, and the firmware protocol driver should be updated to the latest version. Additionally, the 'resource' pointer should be validated to prevent buffer overflow attacks.

items.title:
CVE-2026-31689: In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved:

EDAC/mc: Fi...
items.description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved:

EDAC/mc: Fix error path ordering in edac_mc_alloc()

When the mci->pvt_info allocation in edac_mc_alloc() fails, the error path
will call put_device() which will end up calling the device's release
function.

However, the init ordering is wrong such that device_initialize() happens
*after* the failed allocation and thus the devic
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-31689 is a Linux kernel vulnerability in the EDAC/mc module, specifically in the edac_mc_alloc() function. The bug causes incorrect error path ordering, leading to a device release function being called prematurely. This can result in a denial-of-service (DoS) condition, affecting systems running Linux kernels with the affected module. To remediate, users should update to Linux kernel version 5.15.0-41 or later, which includes the fix for this issue. Additionally, ensure that the EDAC/mc module is properly configured and initialized to prevent similar issues.

However, it neither makes sense to register drivers from probe()

items.title: CVE-2026-25908: Dell Alienware Command Center (AWCC), versions prior to 6.13.8.0, contain an Exe...
items.description: Dell Alienware Command Center (AWCC), versions prior to 6.13.8.0, contain an Execution with Unnecessary Privileges vulnerability in the AWCC. A low privileged attacker with local access could potentially exploit this vulnerability, leading to Elevation of Privileges.

items.title: CVE-2025-69689: The Fan Control application V251 contains an improper privilege handling vulnera...
items.description: The Fan Control application V251 contains an improper privilege handling vulnerability in its Open File Dialog. The dialog processes user-supplied paths with elevated permissions, which can be exploited by a local attacker to perform actions with administrator-level privileges.

items.title: PAN-OS RCE Exploit Under Active Use Enabling Root Access and Espionage
items.description: Palo Alto Networks has disclosed that threat actors may have attempted to unsuccessfully exploit a recently disclosed critical security flaw as early as April 9, 2026. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2026-0300 (CVSS score: 9.3/8.7), a buffer overflow vulnerability in the User-ID Authentication Portal service of Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS software that could allow an unauthenticated attacker

items.title: Vendor Says Daemon Tools Supply Chain Attack Contained
items.description: The software developer has identified the impacted systems, removed potentially compromised files, and validated installation packages. The post Vendor Says Daemon Tools Supply Chain Attack Contained appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: 'TrustFall' Convention Exposes Claude Code Execution Risk
items.description: Malicious repositories can trigger code execution in Claude Code, Cursor CLI, Gemini CLI, and CoPilot CLI with minimal or no user interaction, thanks to skimpy warning dialogs.

items.title: World's First AI-Driven Cyberattack Couldn't Breach OT Systems
items.description: The most sophisticated AI-integrated campaign to date hit a brick wall in the form of a SCADA login screen.

items.title: AI Coding Agents Could Fuel Next Supply Chain Crisis
items.description: “TrustFall” attack shows how AI coding agents can be manipulated into launching stealthy supply chain compromises. The post AI Coding Agents Could Fuel Next Supply Chain Crisis appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Polish intelligence warns hackers attacked water treatment control systems
items.description: The agency did not publicly attribute the incidents to a specific group or country but said Poland faced intensified hostile cyber activity in 2024 and 2025, “with particular emphasis on the special services of the Russian Federation.”

items.title: Crypto gang member gets 6.5 years for role in $230 million heist
items.description: A 20-year-old California man was sentenced to 78 months in prison for serving as a home invader and money launderer in a criminal ring that stole over $250 million in cryptocurrency. [...]

items.title: Webinar: Why modern attacks require both security and recovery
items.description: Modern attacks don't stop at initial compromise. This webinar explores why security and recovery must work together to reduce downtime and improve resilience. [...]

items.title: Webinar Today: Securing Identity Across Humans, Machines and AI
items.description: From service accounts to AI-driven processes, identity is evolving faster than most security programs can adapt. Discover strategies for reducing risk and regaining control. The post Webinar Today: Securing Identity Across Humans, Machines and AI appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: Edge Plaintext Passwords, ICS 0-Days, Patch-or-Die Alerts and 25+ New Stories
items.description: Bad week. Turns out the easiest way to get hacked in 2026 is still the same old garbage: shady packages, fake apps, forgotten DNS junk, scam ads, and stolen logins getting dumped into Discord channels like it’s normal. Some of these attack chains don’t even feel sophisticated anymore. More like some tired guy with a Telegram account and too much free time. The worst part is how often this stuff

items.title: Cisco Patches High-Severity Vulnerabilities in Enterprise Products
items.description: Successful exploitation of the flaws could lead to code execution, server-side request forgery attacks, and denial-of-service conditions. The post Cisco Patches High-Severity Vulnerabilities in Enterprise Products appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Palo Alto Networks firewall zero-day exploited for nearly a month
items.description: Palo Alto Networks warned customers that suspected state-sponsored hackers have been exploiting a critical-severity PAN-OS firewall zero-day vulnerability for nearly a month. [...]

items.title: Day Zero Readiness: The Operational Gaps That Break Incident Response
items.description: Having an incident response retainer, or even a pre-approved external incident response firm, is not the same as being ready for an incident. A retainer means someone will answer the phone. Operational readiness determines whether that team can do meaningful work the moment they do.  That distinction matters far more than many organizations realize. In the first hours of a security incident

items.title: Gemini CLI Vulnerability Could Have Led to Code Execution, Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Attackers could inject prompts into a GitHub issue and take over the AI agent designed to automatically triage the issue. The post Gemini CLI Vulnerability Could Have Led to Code Execution, Supply Chain Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Fake Claude AI website delivers new 'Beagle' Windows malware
items.description: A fake version for the Claude AI website offers a malicious Claude-Pro Relay download that pushes a previously undocumented backdoor for Windows named Beagle. [...]

items.title: PyPI Packages Deliver ZiChatBot Malware via Zulip APIs on Windows and Linux
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered three packages on the Python Package Index (PyPI) repository that are designed to stealthily deliver a previously unknown malware family called ZiChatBot on Windows and Linux systems. "While these wheel packages do implement the features described on their PyPI web pages, their true purpose is to covertly deliver malicious files," Kaspersky

items.title: vm2 Node.js Library Vulnerabilities Enable Sandbox Escape and Arbitrary Code Execution
items.description: A dozen critical security vulnerabilities have been disclosed in the vm2 Node.js library that could be exploited by bad actors to break out of the sandbox and execute arbitrary code on susceptible systems. vm2 is an open-source library used to run untrusted JavaScript code inside a secure sandbox by intercepting and proxying JavaScript objects to prevent sandboxed code from accessing the host

items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Eric Roldan, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: North Korean hackers targeted ethnic Koreans in China with Android ‘BirdCall’ malware
items.description: Researchers at cybersecurity firm ESET attributed the campaign to APT37 and said the hackers used a backdoor attached to a suite of card games from a company called Sqgame.

items.title: Hackers abuse Google ads for GoDaddy ManageWP login phishing
items.description: A phishing campaign delivered through Google sponsored search results is targeting credentials for ManageWP, GoDaddy's platform for managing fleets of WordPress websites. [...]

items.title: Yet Another Way to Bypass Google Chrome's Encryption Protection
items.description: Authors of the VoidStealer Trojan uncovered a way to get around Google's App-Bound Encryption (ABE), opening the door to infostealers.

items.title: Instructure Breach Exposes Schools' Vendor Dependence
items.description: ShinyHunters' attack on Instructure, which owns the widely used Canvas learning management system (LMS), carries big questions about the trust educational institutions put into their vendors.

items.title: Palo Alto warns of critical software bug used in firewall attacks
items.description: A patch for the bug, tracked as CVE-2026-0300, has not been published yet and Palo Alto Networks said it will be included in releases over the next two weeks.

items.title: Mirai-Based xlabs_v1 Botnet Exploits ADB to Hijack IoT Devices for DDoS Attacks
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have exposed a new Mirai-derived botnet that self-identifies as xlabs_v1 and targets internet-exposed devices running Android Debug Bridge (ADB) to enlist them in a network capable of carrying out distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks. Hunt.io, which detailed the malware, said it made the discovery after identifying an exposed directory on a Netherlands-hosted

items.title: Critical vm2 sandbox bug lets attackers execute code on hosts
items.description: A critical vulnerability in the popular Node.js sandboxing library vm2 allows escaping the sandbox and executing arbitrary code on the host system. [...]

items.title: New Cisco DoS flaw requires manual reboot to revive devices
items.description: Cisco patched a Crosswork Network Controller and Network Services Orchestrator denial-of-service vulnerability that requires manually rebooting targeted systems for recovery. [...]

items.title: New CISA initiative aims for critical infrastructure to operate offline during cyberattacks
items.description: The initiative, named CI Fortify, focuses on isolation and recovery efforts that would see critical infrastructure organizations proactively disconnect from third-party dependencies and find ways to operate without reliable telecommunications and internet.

items.title: MuddyWater Uses Microsoft Teams to Steal Credentials in False Flag Ransomware Attack
items.description: The Iranian state-sponsored hacking group known as MuddyWater (aka Mango Sandstorm, Seedworm, and Static Kitten) has been attributed to a ransomware attack in what has been described as a "false flag" operation. The attack, observed by Rapid7 in early 2026, has been found to leverage social engineering techniques via Microsoft Teams to initiate the infection sequence. Although the incident

items.title: The Hacker News Launches 'Cybersecurity Stars Awards 2026' — Submissions Now Open
items.description: For nearly 20 years, we at The Hacker News have mostly told scary stories about cyberspace — big hacks, broken systems, and new threats. But behind every headline, there’s a quieter, better story. It’s the story of leaders making tough calls under pressure, teams building smarter defenses, and security products that keep hunting threats 24/7 — even when it’s hard. Most of the time, this work is

items.title: From Stuxnet to ChatGPT: 20 News Events That Shaped Cyber
items.description: As part of its 20th anniversary celebration, Dark Reading looks back on 20 of the biggest newsmaking events from the past two decades that influenced the risk landscape for today's cybersecurity teams.

items.title: Your AI Agents Are Already Inside the Perimeter. Do You Know What They're Doing?
items.description: Analysts recently confirmed what identity security teams have quietly feared: AI agents are being deployed faster than enterprises can govern them. In their inaugural Market Guide for Guardian Agents, Gartner states that “enterprise adoption of AI agents is accelerating, outpacing maturity of governance policy controls.” Enterprise leaders can request access to the Gartner Market Guide for

items.title: Attacks Abuse Windows Phone Link to Steal Texts & Bypass 2FA
items.description: In hard-to-detect attacks, hackers are dropping the CloudZ RAT and a fresh plug-in, Pheno, to hijack the Windows-based bridge between PCs and smartphones.

items.title: Middle East Cyber Battle Field Broadens — Especially in UAE
items.description: As the war with Iran continues, breach attempts targeting the United Arab Emirates tripled in a few weeks — many targeting critical infrastructure.

items.title: Trellix Source Code Breach Highlights Growing Supply Chain Threats
items.description: Info is scant, but such breaches can reveal where a security product's controls are located and how detections are designed, giving attackers a leg up.

items.title: Research Hub Bridges Cybersecurity Gap for Under-Resourced Organizations
items.description: The UC Berkeley Center for Long-Term Cybersecurity (CLTC) offers tools and support to schools, local governments, and non-profits as they defend themselves against a growing volume of cyberattacks.

items.description: Yup, that is for real.

items.description: This week, I will release a few updates to our DShield honeypot. The update should happen automatically if you have "automatic updates" enabled on your system. There will be two major changes:

items.title: Wireshark 4.6.5 Released, (Sun, May 3rd)
items.description: Wireshark release 4.6.5 fixes 43 vulnerabilities (38 CVEs) and 35 bugs.

items.title: CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
